title: SPARE-Gain: A Low-Compute Benchmark of Baseline-Relative Routing for Unseen-Perturbation Pseudobulk Prediction
description: SPARE-Gain is a benchmarked low-compute routing study rather than a new perturbation-prediction backbone. It tests whether a conformally calibrated lower bound on baseline-relative RMSE gain can decide when to apply a lightweight residual correction more reliably than uncertainty thresholds, classifier gates, and non-conformal gain regression under matched acceptance rates.
motivation: Unseen-perturbation prediction is already crowded with expressive single-cell models, while recent benchmarking work such as Systema and comparison studies show that simple baselines remain strong and common metrics can overstate progress. A stronger low-compute paper is therefore to benchmark deployment decisions over a frozen cheap baseline and determine whether certified baseline-relative gain offers anything beyond ordinary uncertainty filtering.
proposed_approach: Train two cheap pseudobulk baselines on training perturbations only, freeze the stronger one, and fit a lightweight residual corrector on a training-defined responsive gene panel using leakage-free descriptors and retrieval features. Then compare six routing policies over the same frozen baseline and corrected predictor: always baseline, always correct, classifier gate, uncertainty-threshold gate, non-conformal gain regression, and a conformal lower-bound gain gate that applies correction only when a calibrated lower bound on perturbation-level RMSE gain is positive.

related_work.paper: Systema: a framework for evaluating genetic perturbation response prediction beyond systematic variation
related_work.authors: Ramon Vinas Torne, Maciej Wiatrak, Zoe Piran, Shuyang Fan, Liangze Jiang, Sarah A. Teichmann, Mor Nitzan, Maria Brbic
related_work.difference: Systema motivates the benchmark framing and hard-subset evaluation. SPARE-Gain builds on that evaluation mindset but focuses specifically on routing decisions over a frozen low-compute baseline.

related_work.paper: PRESCRIBE: Predicting Single-Cell Responses with Bayesian Estimation
related_work.authors: Jiabei Cheng, Changxi Chi, Jingbo Zhou, Hongyi Xin, Jun Xia
related_work.difference: PRESCRIBE is the closest biology prior art because it uses uncertainty to filter perturbation predictions. SPARE-Gain is only distinct if baseline-relative gain certification beats generic uncertainty filtering at matched acceptance rates.

related_work.authors: Anna Sokol, Nuno Moniz, Nitesh Chawla
related_work.difference: CSR applies conformal prediction to selective regression with a reject option. SPARE-Gain borrows this selective-regression perspective but targets relative gain against a named baseline rather than abstention for a single regressor.

related_work.authors: Anastasios Nikolas Angelopoulos, Stephen Bates, Adam Fisch, Lihua Lei, Tal Schuster
related_work.difference: Conformal Risk Control shows how conformal calibration can control explicit monotone risks. SPARE-Gain adapts that mindset to certify lower-bounded gain against a frozen baseline, not just interval coverage.

related_work.paper: Learn then Test: Calibrating Predictive Algorithms to Achieve Risk Control
related_work.authors: Anastasios N. Angelopoulos, Stephen Bates, Emmanuel J. Candès, Michael I. Jordan, Lihua Lei
related_work.difference: Learn then Test is relevant for explicit risk-targeted calibration and as a sensitivity baseline when calibration data are limited. SPARE-Gain uses it as methodological grounding, not as direct biology prior art.

related_work.paper: Regression with reject option and application to kNN
related_work.authors: Christophe Denis, Mohamed Hebiri, Ahmed Zaoui
related_work.difference: This is classic reject-option regression prior art. SPARE-Gain differs because it routes between two predictors and certifies improvement over baseline rather than abstaining outright.

related_work.paper: Consistent Estimators for Learning to Defer to an Expert
related_work.difference: Learning-to-defer provides the closest model-routing analogy. SPARE-Gain studies a simpler two-model routing case in biology with conformal calibration of relative gain.

related_work.paper: Predicting cellular responses to complex perturbations in high-throughput screens
related_work.difference: CPA and related perturbation predictors address richer response modeling. SPARE-Gain deliberately narrows scope to a low-compute pseudobulk benchmark over deployment policies.

hypothesis: On unseen perturbation pseudobulk benchmarks, residual correction helps only on a subset of perturbations, and a conformally calibrated lower bound on baseline-relative RMSE gain will produce better accepted-set quality than uncertainty thresholds, classifier gates, and non-conformal gain regression at the same acceptance rates.
success_criteria: At 40 percent acceptance, the conformal gain gate improves mean perturbation-level RMSE gain on accepted perturbations over uncertainty-threshold and non-conformal gain baselines in at least 2 of 3 datasets.
The advantage remains directionally positive on hard subsets such as descriptor-novel perturbations or Norman 0-seen combinations.
Accepted perturbations also improve at least one biologically interpretable metric, such as top-response sign accuracy, responsive-gene rank recovery, or pathway-score agreement.
Results remain directionally stable under sensitivity analyses for calibration split, quantile level, and gain definition.
failure_modes_to_test: The conformal gate collapses to ordinary uncertainty filtering and offers no benefit once acceptance rate is matched.
Apparent gains disappear after stratifying by novelty quartile or hard combination bucket.
Calibration requires too many perturbations or becomes too conservative to be useful under realistic split sizes.
Accepted-set RMSE gains do not translate to better marker-gene or pathway-level biological interpretation.
claim_scope: This idea should be presented as a routing benchmark paper unless the conformal gate clearly outperforms uncertainty filtering and non-conformal gain routing. If it does not, the correct conclusion is a negative result: low-compute routing for pseudobulk unseen-perturbation prediction effectively collapses to standard uncertainty filtering.

evaluation_plan.primary_metric: Mean perturbation-level RMSE gain on accepted perturbations at fixed acceptance rate, with 40 percent acceptance as the main operating point and 20/60 percent as sensitivities.
secondary_metrics: All-gene conformal coverage and interval width
sensitivity_analyses: Vary route-dev versus calibration split and compare fixed-split to cross-fit calibration
Vary lower-quantile target alpha across 0.1, 0.2, and 0.3
Recompute gains using all-gene RMSE, top-DE RMSE, and Pearson-delta objectives
evaluation_plan.calibration_reporting: Report exact calibration perturbation counts per dataset in the main results table; treat datasets with fewer than 20 calibration perturbations as underpowered for decisive conformal claims.

feasibility.runtime: Preprocess each dataset once, use one canonical perturbation split per dataset, and reserve three seeds only for the strongest frozen baseline and final conformal routing variant.
feasibility.why_feasible: The study uses pseudobulk data, additive and ridge baselines, a small residual corrector on at most 256 genes, and lightweight routing models. It avoids training large single-cell generative models from scratch and does not depend on reproducing heavy external baselines.
